report:
# What are the tradeoffs of running local LLMs for autonomous agents?

## Hardware and memory requirements

Running a capable model locally is bounded by available memory. A 7B-parameter model fits comfortably in consumer hardware once quantized to 4-bit, while larger models need a workstation-class GPU or unified-memory machine [1]. Quantization trades a small amount of answer quality for a large drop in memory footprint, which is usually the right call for agent loops that issue many short calls [2].

## Latency and throughput versus hosted APIs

Local inference removes network round-trips, so first-token latency is often lower than a hosted API on a fast machine. Sustained throughput, however, is capped by the local accelerator, so a long fan-out research run can be slower end to end than a hosted frontier model [2]. The practical answer is to reserve the local model for cheap, high-volume steps and escalate only the hard synthesis step.

## Privacy, cost, and offline operation

The strongest argument for local models is that no prompt or document ever leaves the machine, which matters for private notes and credentials [3]. Cost shifts from per-token billing to a one-time hardware purchase, and the system keeps working fully offline once the weights are downloaded [3].

## References

1. Quantization formats for local inference - https://example.test/quantization (fetched 2026-05-31T09:00:00Z)
2. Benchmarking local versus hosted inference - https://example.test/benchmarks (fetched 2026-05-31T09:05:00Z)
3. Privacy and cost of self-hosted models - https://example.test/privacy-cost (fetched 2026-05-31T09:10:00Z)
